title: Evaluating at scale
concept: Use perplexity and automated metrics for developer validation, but rely on human rating sets to assess conversational safety and helpfulness in production.

exercises.prompt: Why is it important to evaluate an AI model before releasing it to the public?

options.text: To ensure the AI is safe, helpful, and high-quality.
To make the AI run twice as fast.
To teach the AI how to write code.

exercises.explain: Before launching to users, developers must evaluate the AI's quality and safety to prevent it from outputting harmful content.

cards.title: Is Your AI Safe to Launch?
cards.body: How do you know if an AI is ready for millions of users? You can't read every response yourself, but relying only on automated scores might miss when the AI is being subtly rude or unhelpful!

cards.title: Evaluating at Scale
cards.body: Evaluating at scale means using two layers of testing. First, we use automated metrics like 'perplexity' to quickly check if the AI writes coherent sentences. Second, we use human rating sets to judge if the AI's actual conversations are safe, polite, and helpful.

cards.title: The Kitchen Test vs. The Taste Test
cards.body: Imagine you are opening a new restaurant. Automated metrics like perplexity are like checking if your kitchen temperature is correct and your ingredients are fresh. But to know if your food actually tastes delicious and welcoming to customers, you need a human tasting panel to eat the dishes.

cards.title: Testing in Action
cards.body: An AI team tests their chatbot. The computer checks 10,000 outputs in seconds to ensure the text isn't gibberish. Next, a group of human reviewers reads a sample of 500 conversations to make sure the AI didn't give dangerous advice or sound rude.

cards.title: The Perfect Score Trap
cards.body: People think that if an AI gets a perfect score on automated math or grammar tests, it is 100% safe to launch. In reality, an AI can write perfect grammar while still giving bad or unsafe advice. Human reviewers are still essential.

cards.title: The Evaluation Balance
cards.body: To build a great AI, you need both automated speed for development and human wisdom for safety.

right.text: Deep checks for helpfulness, safety, and politeness.

left.text: Fast, cheap checks for grammar and sentence flow.

exercises.prompt: Why do developers use automated metrics like perplexity?

options.text: To quickly check if the AI is writing fluent, coherent sentences.
To determine if humans find the AI friendly and polite.
To guarantee the AI never tells a lie.

exercises.explain: Perplexity is an automated calculation that measures how confused an AI is by text, helping developers quickly test if the language model is learning patterns correctly.

exercises.prompt: Automated metrics like perplexity are completely sufficient on their own to guarantee an AI is safe for production.
exercises.explain: Automated metrics are excellent for quick validation, but they cannot fully understand nuance, safety, and helpfulness the way human raters do.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete the evaluation strategy rule.
exercises.sentence: While computers check basic grammar, we rely on human ratings to judge AI safety and helpfulness.
exercises.explain: Automated metrics are great for speed, but only human rating sets can truly judge if a conversational AI is acting safely and helpfully.

exercises.prompt: Which of these represents a human rating evaluation?

options.text: The system calculates a perplexity score of 12.4.
A panel of human reviewers rates the AI's response as 'Very Helpful' and 'Safe'.
The code compiles with zero syntax warnings.

exercises.explain: Option A is a machine score, and Option C is just a compiler check. Option B directly uses real human judgment on conversational quality.

exercises.prompt: Your team is launching a new customer support AI. How should you design your evaluation strategy to ensure it behaves safely and helpfully?

options.text: Use only automated tests because humans are too slow.
Use fast automated metrics first, but rely on human ratings for final safety and helpfulness.
Skip evaluations entirely and launch directly to users.
Use human ratings only to check if the code runs without errors.

exercises.explain: While automated tests check general model coherence, human ratings are the gold standard for judging if a chatbot's conversation is safe, polite, and helpful for real users.
